capabilities.advertised_claim: Circle Calculus can state established zero-sum and prime-circle sumset theorems directly in the native `C_n` vocabulary.
capabilities.audience_interest: Erdos-Ginzburg-Ziv, Cauchy-Davenport, zero-sum witnesses, and finite sumsets.
capabilities.circle_math_expression: Finite circle types `C_n` and `C_p` present the cyclic groups directly, with theorem ids for zero-sum subsequences and prime-circle sumset growth.
capabilities.circle_native_value: The Circle framing makes zero-sum witnesses, sharpness families, and sumsets inspectable as finite address-space examples linked to Lean and Python sidecars.

portfolio_routes.audience: Mathematicians who want to know whether Circle Calculus can speak established theorem language.
portfolio_routes.not_claimed: This route does not claim new solutions to open Erdős problems; it demonstrates proof-backed standard-math parity and conservative cyclic reformulations.
portfolio_routes.route_claim: Start with Erdős-adjacent additive combinatorics, extremal set theory, Ramsey theory, and cyclic graph geometry, then inspect the theorem ids, papers, and executable examples behind each bridge.

circle_native_value_refs.circle_math_expression: Finite circle types `C_n` and `C_p` present the cyclic groups directly, with theorem ids for zero-sum subsequences and prime-circle sumset growth.
circle_native_value_refs.circle_native_value: The Circle framing makes zero-sum witnesses, sharpness families, and sumsets inspectable as finite address-space examples linked to Lean and Python sidecars.

circle_native_value_refs.circle_math_expression: Numberings, prefix-density counts, and intersecting families are exposed as theorem-backed finite-circle-method artifacts.
circle_native_value_refs.circle_native_value: The project can show the circle-method proof atom as an executable finite count, then connect it to a formal EKR theorem card.

circle_native_value_refs.circle_math_expression: Dense finite interval examples and AP witness searches are connected to formal Roth theorem handles.
circle_native_value_refs.circle_native_value: Circle Math supplies a bridge toward cyclic AP models while keeping finite witness search, density claims, and Lean theorem status separated.

circle_native_value_refs.circle_math_expression: Finite coloring examples and combinatorial-line widgets can be attached to Lean theorem ids for the general Ramsey statements.
circle_native_value_refs.circle_native_value: The Circle presentation can turn unavoidable structure into inspectable finite search artifacts without confusing examples for proofs.

circle_native_value_refs.circle_math_expression: The one-step finite circle graph is presented as a circulant graph, with connectedness and a minimal unit-distance embedding constructor.
circle_native_value_refs.circle_native_value: The graph lab makes cyclic graph structure explicit before moving to harder Euclidean incidence questions.

sections.evidence: Mathematicians who want to know whether Circle Calculus can speak established theorem language.
sections.label: audience and route signal

sections.evidence: papers 5; theorem refs 11; source refs 11; executables 5; Living Book pages 1
sections.label: proof-backed evidence counts

sections.evidence: This route does not claim new solutions to open Erdős problems; it demonstrates proof-backed standard-math parity and conservative cyclic reformulations.
sections.label: advertising boundary

standard_interest_refs.audience_interest: Erdos-Ginzburg-Ziv, Cauchy-Davenport, zero-sum witnesses, and finite sumsets.
standard_interest_refs.standard_math_anchor: Classical zero-sum and additive-combinatorics theorems over cyclic groups, especially EGZ and Cauchy-Davenport.

standard_interest_refs.audience_interest: Intersecting families, cyclic-order double counting, and sharp extremal examples.
standard_interest_refs.standard_math_anchor: The Katona circle method and the Erdos-Ko-Rado theorem for intersecting uniform set families.

standard_interest_refs.audience_interest: Dense finite sets, arithmetic progressions, Roth numbers, and witness search.
standard_interest_refs.standard_math_anchor: Roth's theorem and the asymptotic sublinearity of maximal three-term-AP-free subsets of natural intervals.

standard_interest_refs.audience_interest: Unavoidable monochromatic structure, combinatorial lines, and finite coloring examples.
standard_interest_refs.standard_math_anchor: Hales-Jewett and Van der Waerden style unavoidable monochromatic-line or homothetic-copy theorems.

standard_interest_refs.audience_interest: Cycle graphs, circulant graphs, connectedness, and unit-distance vocabulary.
standard_interest_refs.standard_math_anchor: Standard cycle graphs, circulant graphs, graph connectedness, and unit-distance embedding vocabulary.

summary_lines: Audience: Mathematicians who want to know whether Circle Calculus can speak established theorem language.
Capability surface: 5 route lanes across Additive combinatorics, Density additive combinatorics, Extremal set theory, Graph geometry, Ramsey theory.
Proof backing: 11 proved theorem refs, 5 paper ids, 11 source refs, 5 executable refs, and 1 Living Book page refs.
Circle-native value: 5 lanes state explicit Circle expression/value statements with role-backed checks.
Boundary: This route does not claim new solutions to open Erdős problems; it demonstrates proof-backed standard-math parity and conservative cyclic reformulations.

portfolio_routes.title: Hard mathematics bridge

portfolio_routes.audience: Readers who want the internal algebra that makes the notation useful rather than decorative.
portfolio_routes.not_claimed: This route does not claim to replace modular arithmetic or standard computation theory; it packages them as proof-carrying cyclic interfaces.
portfolio_routes.route_claim: Follow modular periods, gcd orbits, kernels, fibers, normal forms, circular buffers, stride layouts, and schedule laws as Lean-backed finite cyclic structure.

circle_native_value_refs.circle_math_expression: The S1 theorem spine expresses these facts as finite-circle period, orbit, scaling, image, kernel, and fiber theorems.
circle_native_value_refs.circle_native_value: Circle Math organizes modular facts around movement, closure, fibers, and representatives, making them easier to browse and test.

circle_native_value_refs.circle_math_expression: Compute layouts are expressed as finite address normalization, stride-address, and schedule-preservation theorems.
circle_native_value_refs.circle_native_value: The Circle form can certify when cyclic structure is real enough to justify backend experiments, while requiring benchmarks for performance claims.

sections.evidence: Readers who want the internal algebra that makes the notation useful rather than decorative.
sections.label: audience and route signal

portfolio_routes.audience: Geometry, robotics, and physics-facing readers interested in hidden phase, spin sign, holonomy, and path certificates.
portfolio_routes.not_claimed: This route does not claim continuum physics, full smooth Hopf formalization, or model discovery.
portfolio_routes.route_claim: Use bounded quaternion/Hopf phase facts and proof-carrying finite gauge paths to see where Circle vocabulary clarifies phase and endpoint bookkeeping.

circle_native_value_refs.circle_math_expression: Quaternion, spin-sign, pure-vector, real Hopf-coordinate, and common-phase invariance facts are exposed through theorem spines with explicit topology warnings.
circle_native_value_refs.circle_native_value: The Circle framing separates visible base coordinates from hidden phase in a way that is useful for teaching, widgets, and proof audits.

circle_native_value_refs.circle_math_expression: Finite gauge links and paths are represented with Lean-checked source-target and holonomy laws.
circle_native_value_refs.circle_native_value: Circle Math contributes proof-carrying finite records that can guard simulations and widgets against invalid path composition.

portfolio_routes.title: Phase, rotation, and finite physics

portfolio_routes.audience: Builders who want generated artifacts, cyclic AI schedules, and compute layouts to carry checkable provenance before performance or quality claims.
portfolio_routes.not_claimed: This route does not claim universal compression, model-quality improvement, speedup, or context-length improvement without benchmark evidence.
portfolio_routes.route_claim: Connect seed-rule provenance, exact regeneration gates, cyclic AI memory and route budgets, adapter indexing, and circular compute schedules into reusable proof-carrying systems interfaces.

circle_native_value_refs.circle_math_expression: Seed rules, generator comparisons, proof glyphs, and exactness gates are expressed as finite Circle records and Lean facts.
circle_native_value_refs.circle_native_value: This is a Circle-native value proposition: generated artifacts can carry exact source trails and negative-case checks instead of loose visual claims.

circle_native_value_refs.circle_math_expression: AI-facing memory slots, loop budgets, exit certificates, looped recurrent states, middle-block routes, combined route/budget schedules, strided, hybrid, and stride-family sparse-attention reachability, RoPE relative phase, MultiCoil phase-pair product-cycle closure, residue-plus-winding position records, circulant mixer laws, block-cyclic mixer cells, and adapter blocks are encoded as finite cyclic address/schedule theorems.
circle_native_value_refs.circle_native_value: Circle Math can make experimental cyclic AI components auditable before any model-quality or speed claim is made.

circle_native_value_refs.circle_math_expression: Compute layouts are expressed as finite address normalization, stride-address, and schedule-preservation theorems.
circle_native_value_refs.circle_native_value: The Circle form can certify when cyclic structure is real enough to justify backend experiments, while requiring benchmarks for performance claims.
